does: This paper introduces a human-validated framework to assess whether model-generated reasoning traces actually support their stated conclusions across different languages. The authors analyze 65,000 reasoning traces from GlobalMMLU questions spanning six languages and six frontier LLMs, discovering a critical misalignment: models often achieve high task accuracy while their reasoning fails to logically support their answers. They document that reasoning in non-Latin scripts exhibits at least twice the misalignment compared to Latin scripts. Through human annotation, they develop an error taxonomy identifying that failures stem primarily from evidential errors (unsupported claims, ambiguous facts) and illogical reasoning steps, revealing gaps in current multilingual evaluation methodologies.
connects: This work builds on chain-of-thought prompting research showing that models generate reasoning steps to improve performance, extending these findings to multilingual settings. It responds to growing concerns about whether language models truly reason versus producing plausible-sounding but invalid chains. The research connects to parallel work on hallucinations and factual errors in LLMs, and to emerging multilingual benchmarks like MMLLU that test capabilities across languages. It addresses gaps in reasoning-focused multilingual evaluation that prior work left underexplored, combining insights from error analysis traditions in NLP with recent work on quantifying reasoning quality.
enables: This work points toward reasoning-aware multilingual benchmarking that goes beyond accuracy metrics to validate logical consistency. It suggests future directions for diagnosing and mitigating reasoning failures across language families, particularly for non-Latin scripts where problems are most severe. The error taxonomy could inform training approaches to improve logical consistency in multilingual reasoning. Results motivate developing better evaluation methods that distinguish between task accuracy and reasoning validity, potentially driving improvements in model transparency and trustworthiness. The findings also raise questions about whether current training data and methods adequately support reasoning in lower-resourced language communities.
